data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Caterpillar Sees Bigger Tariff Hit
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Caterpillar raised the expected annual and quarterly impact of tariffs on its results. (0:15) Marvell Technology slumps on disappointing outlook. (0:58) Venture capitalist to be interim head of CDC. (2:02)

The following is an abridged transcrtipt:

Caterpillar (NYSE:CAT) is boosting its expected impact of tariffs.

Shares are under pressure premarket after the company said it now expects the net impact from tariffs to be $1.5 billion to $1.8 billion this year, up from a previous estimate of $1.3 billion to $1.5 billion. For Q3 it sees an impact of $500 million to $600 million, up from earlier outlook of $400 million to $500 million.

Including the net impact from incremental tariffs, Caterpillar said in filing that it now expects its full-year adjusted operating profit margin to come in near the bottom of the target margin range. But it left its full-year sales outlook unchanged.

"While the company continues to take initial mitigating actions to reduce this impact, trade and tariff negotiations continue to be fluid," the company said.

In a further worry to the AI trade, Marvell Technology (MRVL) is tumbling before the bell following disappointing revenue guidance.

For the quarter in progress, Marvell expects a net revenue midpoint of $2.06 billion, less than the $2.11 billion consensus. Adjusted EPS for the third quarter is forecast at $0.74, just above the estimate of $0.73.

For fiscal Q2, the semiconductor company reported adjusted EPS of $0.67, which mirrored the consensus estimate of $0.67. Revenue for the quarter came in at $2.006 billon, which just missed the estimate of $2.01 billion.

Q2 revenue jumped 58% year over year. The gains were led by its data center segment, which surged 69% year over year to $1.49 billion.

But data center revenue is expected to be flat sequentially in Q3.

On the earnings call, CEO Matthew Murphy said “lumpiness” in the custom design business is “normal to see, particularly with the large hyperscale builds that happen and especially as you ramp them into production, which we've done this year on a number of programs.”

Investors “should expect a strong fourth quarter for custom," he added.

And reports say the White House has picked Jim O'Neill, the deputy secretary at Robert F. Kennedy, Jr.'s Department of Health and Human Services, to serve as interim CDC director.

The top CDC post was vacated after the White House said Director Susan Monarez was removed from that office.

O'Neill has an investor background. He was CEO of the Thiel Foundation from 2009 to 2012 and managing director at venture capital firm Mithril Capital Management from 2012 to 2019. Although he has never worked at CDC nor has a public health background, O'Neill has served at HHS in prior Republican administrations. Roles included associate deputy secretary and principal associate deputy secretary.

The AP says a flashpoint is expected in the coming weeks as a key advisory committee, which Kennedy has reshaped with vaccine skeptics, is expected to issue new recommendations on immunizations. The panel is scheduled to review standard childhood shots for measles, hepatitis and other diseases.

Now Here’s What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Alibaba (BABA) reported quarterly earnings that missed on the top and bottom lines.

UnitedHealth (UNH) is under pressure for loans it made to healthcare providers after its cyberattack.

And NBC will offer its Peacock Plus streaming service on Amazon Prime.

Taking a look at the markets, stock index futures are giving some back after the S&P 500 closed above 6,500 for the first time. Tech is weighing after Marvell’s results. In the Treasury market, yields are slightly higher across the curve ahead of the release of Fed’s favorite inflation gauge.

And on the economic calendar today:

  • 8:30 a.m. ET – The July Personal Income and Spending report arrives. That comes with the aforementioned Fed’s favorite inflation gauge, the core PCE price index, which is expected to have risen 0.3% on the month.
  • 8:30 a.m. ET – July Wholesale and Retail Inventories.
  • 9:45 a.m. ET – August Chicago PMI.
  • 10:00 a.m. ET – August Michigan Consumer Sentiment Final.

data.title: Amazon: Snatch Up This Deep Value Growth Stock Now
data.description:
As an investor, my objective is simple: Buy the most qualitative businesses on the planet.

This is done because of the idea that only a select few stocks drive all of the wealth created in the stock market. More specifically, research by Arizona State University professor Hendrik Bessembinder backs up this claim.

Bessembinder found that from 1926 through 2019, approximately 4% of more than 26,000 stocks accounted for all of the wealth creation in the stock market over that time. Strikingly, the remaining 96% of stocks overall only matched the performance of one-month T-bills.

My focus today will be on the retail e-commerce and cloud computing heavyweight Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN)(TSX:AMZN:CA). When I last covered the stock with a strong buy rating in May, I appreciated the role that it plays in our daily lives. I also thought that the shift in shift playing out in global IT spending was a growth catalyst. In my view, the company's AA-rated balance sheet was another plus. Relative to my fair value estimate, shares of the stock were also an undeniable value.

Three months later, I'm reaffirming my strong buy rating. Putting my money where my keyboard is, I'm also going to be adding to my stake in the coming days.

That's because global IT spending allocations could be flipped on their head in the next 10 to 15 years. As the leader in cloud computing, Amazon is strategically positioned to keep cashing in on this secular tailwind. The company's leading e-commerce platform is poised to benefit from industry catalysts, too. Amazon's balance sheet remains robust as well. Finally, the stock is still trading at a sizable discount to my fair value estimate.

Amazon Is Firing On All Cylinders

On July 31st, Amazon shared its financial results for the second quarter ended June 30th, 2025. The company's net sales jumped 13.3% year-over-year to $167.7 billion during the quarter. For more perspective, this topped Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter by $5.6 billion.

Unsurprisingly, Amazon displayed strength throughout its business for the second quarter. The North America segment led the way again, with net sales climbing 11.1% over the year-ago period to $100.1 billion during the quarter. Just like in previous quarters, increased unit sales, advertising sales, and subscription services were credited.

Greater unit sales were again fueled by the trifecta of Amazon's commitment to providing customers with competitive prices, an extensive selection of goods, and convenience.

On the price front, Amazon's value proposition remains industry-leading. CEO Andy Jassy noted in his opening remarks during the Q2 2025 Earnings Call that research firm Profitero has concluded that the company has the lowest prices of any U.S. retailer. This marked the eighth consecutive year the research firm has had this finding.

Amazon has also been working diligently to expand its product selection even further. In recent months, the company has secured the return of Nike's products to the retail store. Amazon has also added premium brands like Aveda and Marc Jacobs Fragrances.

The company has also worked to improve its delivery speeds. In Q2 2025, Amazon delivered 30% more items to Prime members in the U.S., same day or next day, than in Q2 2024.

Perhaps, it's no surprise then that this year's Prime Day last month was another smashing success. It was Amazon's biggest ever with record sales, number of items sold, and Prime sign-ups in the 3 weeks leading up to the event.

The company's International segment posted $36.8 billion in net sales in the second quarter. That was a 16.1% year-over-year growth rate. This outsized growth was largely for the same reasons as the double-digit percentage topline growth rate in the North America segment.

The AWS segment recorded $30.9 billion in net sales for the second quarter, which was up 17.5% over the year-ago period. This was driven by increased customer usage during the quarter, which more than offset pricing changes driven by long-term customer contracts.

Amazon's diluted EPS rocketed 35.5% higher year-over-year to $1.68 in the second quarter. That surpassed the analyst consensus for the quarter by $0.35. This stemmed from a 170-plus basis point expansion in the net profit margin to 10.8% during the quarter.

The big picture remains quite promising for Amazon on the cloud computing front.

CEO Andy Jassy reiterated that 85% to 90% of worldwide IT spend remains on-premises versus in the cloud. Over the next 10 to 15 years, he believes this will flip, with adoption being accelerated by the appetite for leveraging AI.

The future for the AWS segment remains bright. On a company-specific level, demand remains exceptional for Amazon. Fellow SA analyst Yiannis Zourmpanos noted in this article that the company's AWS backlog soared 25% over the year-ago period to $195 billion in the quarter. As long-term trends continue to play out and Amazon works to resolve supply constraints, growth should remain healthy for AWS.

Trends in the global retail e-commerce market should be another driver for Amazon. Grand View Research anticipates that the global retail e-commerce market will compound by 11.6% annually from $5.9 trillion in 2023 to $12.3 trillion by 2030.

As the company continues to enhance the customer experience with low prices, an expanding selection, and faster shipping times, there's every reason to believe it can keep winning share.

This is why I believe that the FAST Graphs analyst growth consensus for operating cash flow per share is realistic. For 2025, the current projection is for OCF per share to rise by 21.3% to $13.11. In 2026, an additional 23.7% spike in OCF per share to $16.21 is expected. For 2027, another 22.7% increase in OCF per share to $19.90 is being predicted.

Still Churning Out Free Cash Flow With A Powerful Balance Sheet

Predictably, Amazon's rapid acceleration in capex spending caused TTM free cash flow to drop 65.7% year-over-year to $18.2 billion as of Q2 2025. Given that the company has many growth avenues, it makes sense that it's aggressively investing in its future. If/when Amazon chooses to flip the free cash flow switch, I don't doubt that it can do so with ease.

Financially, Amazon's net cash and cash equivalents/marketable securities continue to grow. As of June 30th, 2025, this balance was $42.5 billion. This was up sequentially from $41.2 billion in Q1 2025. Consequently, Amazon earns an AA credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook (unless otherwise noted or hyperlinked, all details in the prior two subheads were from Amazon's Q2 2025 Earnings Press Release, Amazon's Q2 2025 10-Q Filing, and Amazon's Q2 2025 Earnings Presentation).

Fair Value Keeps Climbing Higher

In the last three months, Amazon's shares have generated a 12% total return. That's just ahead of the 10% total return logged by the S&P 500 index (SP500) over that time.

Amazon's outperformance looks like it could be just beginning, too. That's because the stock remains objectively cheap based on the valuation metric in which it tracks most in line. Of course, I'm referring to Amazon's valuation on an operating cash flow basis.

The stock's forward 12-month P/OCF ratio is only 15.1. Even after its recent rally, this is still almost four standard deviations less than the 10-year average P/OCF ratio of 23.8, per FAST Graphs.

As I have discussed in previous articles, I believe that a fair value P/OCF ratio for Amazon is 21.4. This would be one standard deviation under the 10-year average multiple.

Why am I reasonably confident that this represents Amazon's fair value P/OCF ratio?

Well, the company's annual OCF per share growth consensus of 22.7% remains close to its 10-year average OCF per share CAGR of around 27%. When adjusting for the sheer size of Amazon now versus five or 10 years ago, it makes sense that percentage growth is slowing. In the grand scheme of things, it's not by all that much, though.

This suggests that Amazon's growth profile remains largely intact. Thus, I believe that a P/OCF ratio of 21.4 appropriately reflects this fact.

Currently, the calendar year 2025 is 66% complete. That means another 34% of 2025 and 66% of 2026 lie ahead in the coming 12 months. This yields a forward 12-month OCF per share input of $15.14.

Plugging this into my fair value P/OCF ratio of 21.4, I compute a fair value of $324 a share. Compared to the current $229 share price (as of August 27th, 2025), that works out to be a 29% discount to fair value.

If Amazon meets growth expectations and reverts to fair value, it could have a 45% upside through September 2026. Over the next five years, the stock could put up a cumulative total return of 217% - - 24% annualized.

Risks To Consider

Amazon enjoys a vigorous growth outlook. Even so, the company does have risks that could alter or undo the investment thesis.

One risk to Amazon is its dominance in U.S. retail e-commerce. The most recent data from Business Dasher shows the company's share of the market is approaching 40% (37.8%). That's magnitudes higher than Walmart's (WMT) 6.3% market share.

This level of superiority is a double-edged sword. On one hand, I love to see my investment holdings tower over competitors. This serves as a testament to Amazon's ability to deliver positive experiences that routinely bring customers back for more.

On the other hand, regulators may not see it this way. The risk is that if a legal challenge arose and Amazon didn't prevail, the company could be broken into separate entities. That could interfere with Amazon's ecosystem and potentially hurt its growth potential.

As I noted in May, another risk to the company is the potential for a cyber breach. Amazon's scores of sensitive data make it the target of countless attempted breaches. If any find widespread success, this could result in sensitive stakeholder data being jeopardized. That could prompt lawsuits and fines from regulators, possibly harming Amazon's brand image and market share.

One last risk to the company is that it is economically sensitive. Economic slowdowns can temporarily weigh on growth prospects and pressure the stock's sentiment. This happened in 2022, when OCF per share only grew by 2% to $4.59.

The Kalshi betting odds for a recession in 2025 have dropped from 47.9% at the start of this year (and a peak near 70% in April) to just 10% now. Still, it's worth noting.

Summary: I'm Buying More Amazon Next Week

The continued shift in IT spending from on-premises to the cloud bodes well for Amazon. As does the ongoing surge in global retail e-commerce. This is why I still think the company has a lengthy runway for double-digit percentage OCF per share growth. Amazon's significant net cash and cash equivalents/marketable securities position adds to its appeal. Shares also remain firmly discounted, which is leading me to stand with my strong buy rating.

I have added to my Amazon position five times in 2025. This has helped the stock to become my second-biggest position behind Nvidia (NVDA), at a 5.8% weighting. As a sign of my conviction, I'm going to be boosting my Amazon stake by nearly another 10% to begin next month.

Commentary as of 06/30/25

  • The fund posted a return of 6.39% (Class I shares) for the second quarter of 2025.
  • The largest contributors to relative performance were investment decisions in the information technology (IT) sector and security selection in the health care and consumer staples sectors. The largest detractors were investment decisions in the industrials sector and security selection in the financials and communication services sectors.
  • The largest exposures were in the financials, health care, and IT sectors. During the quarter, the fund increased its allocations to the IT and communication services sectors, and reduced its exposures to the health care and financials sectors.
Contributors Detractors
Investment decisions in the IT sector contributed the most, specifically in the technology hardware, storage & peripherals industry. Stock selection in the health care sector, particularly in the health care providers & services industry, boosted relative performance. Lastly, selection decisions in the consumer staples sector, specifically in the consumer staples distribution & retail industry, had a positive impact.

The largest detractor was security selection and an underweight position in the industrials sector, notably in the electrical equipment industry. Selection decisions in the financials sector negatively impacted relative performance, particularly in the capital markets industry. Lastly, stock selection in the communication services sector, namely allocation decisions and security selection in the media industry, was unhelpful.

Further insight

At quarter-end, markets remain volatile, shaped by trade and geopolitical tensions, and mixed economic signals. However, optimism about artificial intelligence (AI) remains, with companies emphasizing the technology in earnings calls and delivering strong results. We remain anchored in a bottom-up, fundamentals-driven approach. While corporate earnings are healthy, full valuations demand precision and discipline. In this environment, the fund’s value-oriented strategy, which is focused on companies with strong balance sheets, durable cash flows, and reasonable valuations, offers stability.
